Ordinary Differential Equations

Definition: An ordinary differential equation is a differential equation which relates a function of a single-variable and its derivatives. The most general form of a differential equation is

F(x,y,y′,y′′,y′′′,…,y(n−1),y(n))=0 F(x,y,y',y'',y''',\dots,y^{(n-1)},y^{(n)}) = 0 F(x,y,y′,y′′,y′′′,…,y(n−1),y(n))=0

where nnn is called the order of the differential equation.

Definition: An integral curve ...

Picard’s Theorem

...

Separable Equations

Definition: A separable equation is an equation of the form

y′=f(x)g(y). y' = f(x)g(y). y′=f(x)g(y).

Autonomous Equations

Definition: An autonomous equation is an equation of the form

dydt=g(y). \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} t} = g(y). dtdy​=g(y).

Equilibrium Values

...

Stability

...

Homogeneous Equations

Definition: A homogeneous equation is an equation of the form

y′=f(yx). y' = f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right). y′=f(xy​).

Change of Variables

Exact Equations

Definition: The differential equation M(x,y)+N(x,y)y′=0M(x,y) + N(x,y)y' = 0M(x,y)+N(x,y)y′=0 is called exact if there exists a differentiable function ψ(x,y)\psi(x,y)ψ(x,y) satisfying

ψx(x,y)=M(x,y)andψy(x,y)=N(x,y). \psi_{x}(x,y) = M(x,y)\quad\text{and}\quad\psi_{y}(x,y) = N(x,y). ψx​(x,y)=M(x,y)andψy​(x,y)=N(x,y).

Bernoulli Equations

Definition: A Bernoulli equation is an equation of the form

y′+p(x)y=q(x)yn. y' + p(x)y = q(x)y^{n}. y′+p(x)y=q(x)yn.

Vector Spaces

Definition: A real vector space is a set VVV with the following properties:

  1. ∀u,v∈V,u+v∈V\forall\mathbf{ u}, \mathbf{ v}\in V, \mathbf{ u} + \mathbf{ v}\in V∀u,v∈V,u+v∈V

  2. ∀u∈V,∀c∈R,cu∈V\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, \forall c\in\mathbb{R}, c\mathbf{ u}\in V∀u∈V,∀c∈R,cu∈V

  3. ∀u,v∈V,u+v=v+u\forall\mathbf{ u}, \mathbf{ v}\in V, \mathbf{ u} + \mathbf{ v} = \mathbf{ v} + \mathbf{ u}∀u,v∈V,u+v=v+u

  4. ∀u,v,w∈V,(u+v)+w=u+(v+w)\forall\mathbf{ u}, \mathbf{ v}, \mathbf{ w}\in V, (\mathbf{ u} + \mathbf{ v}) + \mathbf{ w} = \mathbf{ u} + (\mathbf{ v} + \mathbf{ w})∀u,v,w∈V,(u+v)+w=u+(v+w)

  5. ∀u∈V,∃0∈V,u+0=0+u=u\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, \exists\mathbf{ 0}\in V, \mathbf{ u} + \mathbf{ 0} = \mathbf{ 0} + \mathbf{ u} = \mathbf{ u}∀u∈V,∃0∈V,u+0=0+u=u

  6. ∀u∈V,∃−u∈V,u+(−u)=0\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, \exists\mathbf{ -u}\in V, \mathbf{ u} + (-\mathbf{ u}) = \mathbf{ 0}∀u∈V,∃−u∈V,u+(−u)=0

  7. ∀u∈V,1u=u\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, 1\mathbf{ u} = \mathbf{ u}∀u∈V,1u=u

  8. ∀u∈V,∀a,b∈R,a(bu)=(ab)u\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, \forall a, b\in\mathbb{R}, a(b\mathbf{ u}) = (ab)\mathbf{ u}∀u∈V,∀a,b∈R,a(bu)=(ab)u

  9. ∀u,v∈V,∀c∈R,c(u+v)=cu+cv\forall\mathbf{ u}, \mathbf{ v}\in V, \forall c\in\mathbb{R}, c(\mathbf{ u}+\mathbf{ v}) = c\mathbf{ u} + c\mathbf{ v}∀u,v∈V,∀c∈R,c(u+v)=cu+cv

  10. ∀u∈V,∀a,b∈R,(a+b)u=au+bu\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, \forall a, b\in\mathbb{R}, (a+b)\mathbf{ u} = a\mathbf{ u} + b\mathbf{ u}∀u∈V,∀a,b∈R,(a+b)u=au+bu.

Theorem: If VVV is a real vector space, then

  1. the zero vector is unique

  2. ∀u∈V,0u=0\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, 0\mathbf{ u} = \mathbf{ 0}∀u∈V,0u=0

  3. ∀c∈R,c0=0\forall c\in\mathbb{R}, c\mathbf{ 0} = \mathbf{ 0}∀c∈R,c0=0

  4. additive inverses are unique

  5. ∀u∈V,−u=(−1)u\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, -\mathbf{ u} =(-1)\mathbf{ u}∀u∈V,−u=(−1)u

  6. ∀u∈V,∀c∈R\forall\mathbf{ u}\in V, \forall c\in\mathbb{R}∀u∈V,∀c∈R if cu=0c\mathbf{ u} = \mathbf{ 0}cu=0, then u=0\mathbf{ u} = \mathbf{ 0}u=0, c=0c = 0c=0, or both.

Example: The set of all 1×n1 \times n1×n row vectors is a vector space.

Example: The set of all m×1m \times 1m×1 column vectors is a vector space.

Example: The set of all 2×22 \times 22×2 matrices with real entries is a vector space.

Example: Let I⊆RI \subseteq\mathbb{R}I⊆R be an interval. The set of all real-valued functions on III is a vector space.

Example: The set of all solutions of the differential equation y′′+y′+y=0y'' + y' + y = 0y′′+y′+y=0 is a vector space.

Subspaces

Definition: Let VVV be a real vector space. Let WWW be a subset of VVV. Then WWW is a subspace of VVV if WWW itself is a vector space using the same vector addition and scalar multiplication operations of VVV.

For the following examples, suppose VVV is a vector space.

Example: If W⊆VW\subseteq VW⊆V and W={0}W = \{\mathbf{ 0}\}W={0}, then WWW is called the trivial subspace.

Example: If W⊆VW\subseteq VW⊆V and W=VW = VW=V, then WWW is called the whole subspace (also known as the whole space).

Non-Example: If W⊆VW\subseteq VW⊆V and W=∅W = \varnothingW=∅, WWW fails to have a zero vector, therefore it is not a subspace of VVV.

Theorem: If VVV is a vector space and W⊆VW\subseteq VW⊆V, to check if WWW is a subspace of VVV you need only verify three things:

  1. ∀u,v∈W,u+v∈W\forall\mathbf{ u}, \mathbf{ v}\in W, \mathbf{ u} + \mathbf{ v}\in W∀u,v∈W,u+v∈W

  2. ∀u∈W,∀c∈R,cu∈W\forall\mathbf{ u}\in W, \forall c\in\mathbb{R}, c\mathbf{ u}\in W∀u∈W,∀c∈R,cu∈W

  3. ∃0∈W\exists\mathbf{ 0}\in W∃0∈W.

Geometry of Subspaces

Subspaces of R2\mathbb{R}^{2}R2 are either a point on the origin (trivial subspace), a line through the origin, or the entire space.

Subspaces of R3\mathbb{R}^{3}R3 are either a point on the origin, a line through the origin, a plane through the origin, or the entire space.

Spanning Sets

Linear Combinations

Definition: A linear combination of a set of vectors u1,u2,u3,…,un\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \mathbf{ u_{2}}, \mathbf{ u_{3}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}u1​,u2​,u3​,…,un​ is of the form

a1u1+a2u2+a3u3+⋯+anun a_{1}\mathbf{ u_{1}} + a_{2}\mathbf{ u_{2}} + a_{3}\mathbf{ u_{3}} + \dots + a_{n}\mathbf{ u_{n}} a1​u1​+a2​u2​+a3​u3​+⋯+an​un​

where a1,a2,a3,…,an∈Ra_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \dots, a_{n}\in\mathbb{R}a1​,a2​,a3​,…,an​∈R.

Definition: The set of all such linear combinations is called the span of {u1,…,un}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}{u1​,…,un​} and is denoted by

span{u1,…,un}={a1u1+a2u2+a3u3+⋯+anun:a1,…,an∈R}. \text{span}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\} = \{a_{1}\mathbf{ u_{1}} + a_{2}\mathbf{ u_{2}} + a_{3}\mathbf{ u_{3}} + \dots + a_{n}\mathbf{ u_{n}}:a_{1}, \dots, a_{n}\in\mathbb{R}\}. span{u1​,…,un​}={a1​u1​+a2​u2​+a3​u3​+⋯+an​un​:a1​,…,an​∈R}.

Theorem: The span of a set of vectors in VVV is a subspace of VVV.

Definition: If V=span{u1,…,un}V = \text{span}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}V=span{u1​,…,un​}, {u1,…,un}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}{u1​,…,un​} is a spanning set for VVV.

Linear Independence

Consider the equation a1u1+a2u2+a3u3+⋯+anun=0a_{1}\mathbf{ u_{1}} + a_{2}\mathbf{ u_{2}} + a_{3}\mathbf{ u_{3}} + \dots + a_{n}\mathbf{ u_{n}} = \mathbf{ 0}a1​u1​+a2​u2​+a3​u3​+⋯+an​un​=0.

Definition: A set of vectors {u1,…,un}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}{u1​,…,un​} is called linearly independent if the only solution to the equation above is a1=0,a2=0,a3=0,…,an=0a_{1} = 0, a_{2} = 0, a_{3} = 0, \dots, a_{n} = 0a1​=0,a2​=0,a3​=0,…,an​=0 which is also known as the trivial solution.

Definition: A set of vectors {u1,…,un}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}{u1​,…,un​} is called linearly dependent if there exists a solution to the equation above where a1,a2,a3,…,ana_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \dots, a_{n}a1​,a2​,a3​,…,an​ aren't all zero, in other words, a non-trivial solution.

Theorem: Suppose {u1,…,un}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}{u1​,…,un​} is a linearly dependent set of vectors and un\mathbf{ u_{n}}un​ can be written as a linear combination of {u1,…,un−1}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n-1}}\}{u1​,…,un−1​}. Then,

span{u1,…,un}=span{u1,…,un−1}. \text{span}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\} = \text{span}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n-1}}\}. span{u1​,…,un​}=span{u1​,…,un−1​}.

The equation a1u1+a2u2+a3u3+⋯+anun=0a_{1}\mathbf{ u_{1}} + a_{2}\mathbf{ u_{2}} + a_{3}\mathbf{ u_{3}} + \dots + a_{n}\mathbf{ u_{n}} = \mathbf{ 0}a1​u1​+a2​u2​+a3​u3​+⋯+an​un​=0 is equivalent to the system of linear homogeneous equations Aa=0A\mathbf{ a} = \mathbf{ 0}Aa=0 where AAA is the m×nm \times nm×n matrix whose column vectors are u1,…,un\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}u1​,…,un​ and a\mathbf{ a}a is the vector whose entries are a1,…,ana_{1}, \dots, a_{n}a1​,…,an​.

Theorem: Let AAA be the m×nm \times nm×n matrix whose column vectors are u1,…,un\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}u1​,…,un​. Then, {u1,…,un}\{\mathbf{ u_{1}}, \dots, \mathbf{ u_{n}}\}{u1​,…,un​} is linearly independent if and only if the only solution to Aa=0A\mathbf{ a} = \mathbf{ 0}Aa=0 is the trivial solution a=0\mathbf{ a} = \mathbf{ 0}a=0. Equivalently, this is true if and only if the system has no free variables.

The Wrońskian

Definition: Let f1(x),…,fn(x)f_{1}(x), \dots, f_{n}(x)f1​(x),…,fn​(x) be a set of nnn functions which are all differentiable at least n−1n-1n−1 times. The Wrońskian of these functions is the determinant expression

W(f1(x),…,fn(x))=det[f1(x)f2(x)…fn(x)f1′(x)f2′(x)…fn′(x)⋮⋮⋱⋮f1(n−1)(x)f2(n−1)(x)…fn(n−1)(x)]. W(f_{1}(x), \dots, f_{n}(x)) = \text{det}\left[\begin{array}{cccc}f_{1}(x) & f_{2}(x) & \dots & f_{n}(x)\\ f_{1}'(x) & f_{2}'(x) & \dots & f_{n}'(x)\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ f_{1}^{(n-1)}(x) & f_{2}^{(n-1)}(x) & \dots & f_{n}^{(n-1)}(x)\end{array}\right]. W(f1​(x),…,fn​(x))=det⎣⎡​f1​(x)f1′​(x)⋮f1(n−1)​(x)​f2​(x)f2′​(x)⋮f2(n−1)​(x)​……⋱…​fn​(x)fn′​(x)⋮fn(n−1)​(x)​⎦⎤​.

Theorem: Suppose that the Wrońskian W(f1(x),…,fn(x))W(f_{1}(x), \dots, f_{n}(x))W(f1​(x),…,fn​(x)) is non-zero on at least one value in an interval III. Then, the functions f1(x),…,fn(x)f_{1}(x), \dots, f_{n}(x)f1​(x),…,fn​(x) are linearly independent over the interval III.

Note that in the theorem above, this is not an if and only if statement. It is possible for the Wrońskian to be zero and the set of functions to be linearly independent.

Vandermonde Determinant

Definition: Suppose that c1,…,cnc_{1},\dots,c_{n}c1​,…,cn​ are real or complex numbers. The Vandermonde matrix for these numbers is

V(c1,…,cn)=[11…1c1c2…cn⋮⋮⋱⋮c1n−1c2n−1…cnn−1]. V(c_{1},\dots,c_{n}) = \left[\begin{array}{cccc}1 & 1 & \dots & 1\\ c_{1} & c_{2} & \dots & c_{n}\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ c_{1}^{n-1} & c_{2}^{n-1} & \dots & c_{n}^{n-1}\end{array}\right]. V(c1​,…,cn​)=⎣⎡​1c1​⋮c1n−1​​1c2​⋮c2n−1​​……⋱…​1cn​⋮cnn−1​​⎦⎤​.

Definition:

Bases of a Vector Space

Definition: A basis for a vector space is a set of vectors that both span the space and are linearly independent.

Definition: A vector space is called finite dimensional if it has a finite basis and infinite dimensional if every basis is infinite.

Theorem: If SSS is a spanning set of VVV with nnn elements and LLL is a linearly independent subset of VVV, then LLL has at most nnn elements.

Corollary: Suppose that BBB is basis for VVV consisting of nnn elements. Then, any set with less than nnn elements cannot span the space and any set with more than nnn elements cannot be linearly independent.

Dimension of a Vector Space

Definition: If VVV is a finite dimensional vector space, we call the cardinality of its bases the dimension of VVV denoted as dim(V)\text{dim}(V)dim(V).

Theorem: If B1B_{1}B1​ and B2B_{2}B2​ are each a basis for the same vector space VVV and one of them is finite, then both bases are finite and have the same dimension.

Theorem: Suppose VVV is nnn-dimensional. If SSS is a finite spanning set of vectors in VVV, then SSS contains a basis for VVV and SSS has at least nnn elements.

Corollary: If dim(V)=n\text{dim}(V) = ndim(V)=n, then any set of nnn spanning vectors is a basis.

Theorem: Suppose VVV is nnn-dimensional. If SSS is a finite set of linearly independent vectors in VVV, then SSS has at most nnn elements which can be extended to become a basis.

Corollary: If dim(V)=n\text{dim}(V) = ndim(V)=n, then any set of nnn linearly independent vectors is a basis.

Linear Transformations

Consider T:V→WT: V \rightarrow WT:V→W.

TTT is a map where VVV is the domain and WWW is the codomain. Linear transformations (also known as linear mappings) are special types of maps.

Definition: A linear transformation is a function T:V→WT: V \rightarrow WT:V→W with the properties that TTT:

  • preserves scalar multiplication, T(cv)=cT(v)T(c\mathbf{ v}) = cT(\mathbf{ v})T(cv)=cT(v)

  • preserves vector addition, T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v)T(\mathbf{ u}+\mathbf{ v}) = T(\mathbf{ u}) + T(\mathbf{ v})T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v).

Example: D:Cn(I)→Cn−1(I)D: C^{n}(I) \rightarrow C^{n-1}(I)D:Cn(I)→Cn−1(I), D(f(x))=f′(x)D(f(x))=f'(x)D(f(x))=f′(x)

Note: III is an interval and Cn(I)C^{n}(I)Cn(I) is a set of functions on III with at least nnn derivatives and fn(x)f^{n}(x)fn(x) is continuous.

To prove DDD is a linear transformation, we need to show DDD satisfies three things:

  • it is well-defined

  • preserves scalar multiplication

  • preserves vector addition.

Check 1) Well-defined

Take f(x)∈Cn(I)f(x)\in C^{n}(I)f(x)∈Cn(I). Then, f′(x),f′′(x),f′′′(x),…,fn(x)f'(x), f''(x), f'''(x), \dots, f^{n}(x)f′(x),f′′(x),f′′′(x),…,fn(x) all exist and are continuous on III. Therefore, f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) exists and f′(x)∈Cn−1(I)f'(x)\in C^{n-1}(I)f′(x)∈Cn−1(I) is equal to f′(x)∈Cn−1(I)f'(x)\in C^{n-1}(I)f′(x)∈Cn−1(I) and is well-defined.

Check 2) Scalar multiplication

Take f(x)∈Cn(I)f(x)\in C^{n}(I)f(x)∈Cn(I) and c∈Rc\in\mathbb{R}c∈R. Then,

D(cf(x))=(cf(x))′=cf′(x)=cD(f(x))\begin{aligned} D(cf(x)) &= (cf(x))'\\ &= cf'(x)\\ &= cD(f(x)) \end{aligned}D(cf(x))​=(cf(x))′=cf′(x)=cD(f(x))​

so, D(cf(x))=cD(f(x))D(cf(x)) = cD(f(x))D(cf(x))=cD(f(x)).

Check 3) Vector addition

Take f(x),g(x)∈Cn(I)f(x), g(x)\in C^{n}(I)f(x),g(x)∈Cn(I). Then,

D(f(x)+g(x))=(f(x)+g(x))′=f′(x)+g′(x)=D(f(x))+D(g(x))\begin{aligned} D(f(x)+g(x)) &= (f(x)+g(x))'\\ &= f'(x) + g'(x)\\ &= D(f(x)) + D(g(x)) \end{aligned}D(f(x)+g(x))​=(f(x)+g(x))′=f′(x)+g′(x)=D(f(x))+D(g(x))​

so, D(f(x)+g(x))=D(f(x))+D(g(x))D(f(x)+g(x)) = D(f(x)) + D(g(x))D(f(x)+g(x))=D(f(x))+D(g(x)).

Therefore, DDD is a linear transformation.

Example: T:C2(I)→C0(I),T(f(x))=a2(x)f′′(x)+a1f′(x)+a0(x)f(x)T: C^{2}(I) \rightarrow C^{0}(I), T(f(x)) = a_{2}(x)f''(x)+a_{1}f'(x)+a_{0}(x)f(x)T:C2(I)→C0(I),T(f(x))=a2​(x)f′′(x)+a1​f′(x)+a0​(x)f(x) where a2(x),a1(x),a0(x)∈C0(I)a_{2}(x), a_{1}(x), a_{0}(x)\in C^{0}(I)a2​(x),a1​(x),a0​(x)∈C0(I)

Check 1) Well-defined

f(x),f′(x),f′′(x)f(x), f'(x), f''(x)f(x),f′(x),f′′(x) are continuous so a2(x)f′′(x)+a1f′(x)+a0(x)f(x)a_{2}(x)f''(x) + a_{1}f'(x) + a_{0}(x)f(x)a2​(x)f′′(x)+a1​f′(x)+a0​(x)f(x) is continuous, thus TTT is well-defined.

Check 2) Scalar multiplication

Take f(x)∈C2(I),c∈Rf(x)\in C^{2}(I), c\in\mathbb{R}f(x)∈C2(I),c∈R.

T(cf(x))=a2(x)f′′(cx)+a1f′(cx)+a0(x)f(cx)=ca2(x)f′′(x)+ca1f′(x)+ca0(x)f(x)=c(a2(x)f′′(x)+a1f′(x)+a0(x)f(x))=cT(f(x))\begin{aligned} T(cf(x)) &= a_2(x)f''(cx) + a_1f'(cx) + a_0(x)f(cx)\\ &= ca_2(x)f''(x) + ca_1f'(x) + ca_0(x)f(x)\\ &= c(a_2(x)f''(x) + a_1f'(x) + a_0(x)f(x))\\ &= cT(f(x)) \end{aligned}T(cf(x))​=a2​(x)f′′(cx)+a1​f′(cx)+a0​(x)f(cx)=ca2​(x)f′′(x)+ca1​f′(x)+ca0​(x)f(x)=c(a2​(x)f′′(x)+a1​f′(x)+a0​(x)f(x))=cT(f(x))​

Check 3) Vector addition

Take f(x),g(x)∈C2(I)f(x), g(x)\in C^{2}(I)f(x),g(x)∈C2(I).

T(f(x)+g(x))=a2(x)(f(x)+g(x))′′+a1(f(x)+g(x))′+a0(x)(f(x)+g(x))=a2(x)(f′′(x)+g′′(x))+a1(f′(x)+g′(x))+a0(x)(f(x)+g(x))=a2(x)f′′(x)+a2(x)g′′(x)+a1(x)f′(x)+a1(x)g′(x)+a0(x)f(x)+a0(x)g(x)=a2(x)f′′(x)+a1(x)f′(x)+a0(x)f(x)+a2(x)g′′(x)+a1(x)g′(x)+a0(x)g(x)=T(f(x))+T(g(x))\begin{aligned} T(f(x)+g(x)) &= a_2(x)(f(x)+g(x))'' + a_1(f(x)+g(x))' + a_0(x)(f(x)+g(x))\\ &= a_2(x)(f''(x)+g''(x)) + a_1(f'(x)+g'(x)) + a_0(x)(f(x)+g(x))\\ &= a_2(x)f''(x) + a_2(x)g''(x) + a_1(x)f'(x) + a_1(x)g'(x) + a_0(x)f(x) + a_0(x)g(x)\\ &= a_2(x)f''(x) + a_1(x)f'(x) + a_0(x)f(x) + a_2(x)g''(x) + a_1(x)g'(x) + a_0(x)g(x)\\ &= T(f(x)) + T(g(x)) \end{aligned}T(f(x)+g(x))​=a2​(x)(f(x)+g(x))′′+a1​(f(x)+g(x))′+a0​(x)(f(x)+g(x))=a2​(x)(f′′(x)+g′′(x))+a1​(f′(x)+g′(x))+a0​(x)(f(x)+g(x))=a2​(x)f′′(x)+a2​(x)g′′(x)+a1​(x)f′(x)+a1​(x)g′(x)+a0​(x)f(x)+a0​(x)g(x)=a2​(x)f′′(x)+a1​(x)f′(x)+a0​(x)f(x)+a2​(x)g′′(x)+a1​(x)g′(x)+a0​(x)g(x)=T(f(x))+T(g(x))​

Therefore, TTT is a linear transformation.

Non-Example: T:R2→R2,T((x,y))=(x+1,y+1)T: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}, T((x,y)) = (x+1,y+1)T:R2→R2,T((x,y))=(x+1,y+1)

Consider,

T((2,3))=(2+1,3+1)=(3,4).\begin{aligned} T((2,3)) &= (2+1,3+1)\\ &= (3,4). \end{aligned}T((2,3))​=(2+1,3+1)=(3,4).​

Observe,

T(2(2,3))=T((4,6))=(4+1,6+1)=(5,7)\begin{aligned} T(2(2,3)) &= T((4,6))\\ &= (4+1,6+1)\\ &= (5,7) \end{aligned}T(2(2,3))​=T((4,6))=(4+1,6+1)=(5,7)​

and,

2T((2,3))=2(3,4)=(6,8).\begin{aligned} 2T((2,3)) &= 2(3,4)\\ &= (6,8). \end{aligned}2T((2,3))​=2(3,4)=(6,8).​

Since we have T(2(2,3))≠2T((2,3))T(2(2,3)) \neq 2T((2,3))T(2(2,3))=2T((2,3)), TTT doesn't preserve scalar multiplication, thus it's not a linear transformation.

Example: T:R2→R2,T((x,y))=(x+y,x−y)T: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}, T((x,y)) = (x+y,x-y)T:R2→R2,T((x,y))=(x+y,x−y)

Check 1) Scalar multiplication

Take (x,y)∈R2,c∈R(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}, c\in\mathbb{R}(x,y)∈R2,c∈R.

T(c(x,y))=T((cx,cy))=(cx+cy,cx−cy)=c(x+y,x−y)=cT(x,y)\begin{aligned} T(c(x,y)) &= T((cx,cy))\\ &= (cx+cy,cx-cy)\\ &= c(x+y,x-y)\\ &= cT(x,y) \end{aligned}T(c(x,y))​=T((cx,cy))=(cx+cy,cx−cy)=c(x+y,x−y)=cT(x,y)​

Check 2) Vector addition

Take (x,y),(a,b)∈R2(x,y), (a,b)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}(x,y),(a,b)∈R2.

T((x,y)+(a,b))=T((x+a,y+b))=((x+a)+(y+b),(x+a)−(y+b))=(x+a+y+b,x+a−y−b)=(x+y,x−y)+(a+b,a−b)=T((x,y))+T((a,b))\begin{aligned} T((x,y)+(a,b)) &= T((x+a,y+b))\\ &= ((x+a)+(y+b),(x+a)-(y+b))\\ &= (x+a+y+b,x+a-y-b)\\ &= (x+y,x-y)+(a+b,a-b)\\ &= T((x,y)) + T((a,b)) \end{aligned}T((x,y)+(a,b))​=T((x+a,y+b))=((x+a)+(y+b),(x+a)−(y+b))=(x+a+y+b,x+a−y−b)=(x+y,x−y)+(a+b,a−b)=T((x,y))+T((a,b))​

Therefore, TTT is a linear transformation.

Non-Example: T:R2→R,T(x,y)=x3+y33T: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, T(x,y) = \sqrt[3]{x^3+y^3}T:R2→R,T(x,y)=3x3+y3​

Check 1) Scalar multiplication

Take (x,y)∈R2,c∈R(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{2}, c\in\mathbb{R}(x,y)∈R2,c∈R.

T(c(x,y))=T((cx,cy)))=(cx)3+(cy)33=c3(x3+y3)3=cx3+y33=cT((x,y))\begin{aligned} T(c(x,y)) &= T((cx,cy)))\\ &= \sqrt[3]{(cx)^3+(cy)^3}\\ &= \sqrt[3]{c^3(x^3+y^3)}\\ &= c\sqrt[3]{x^3+y^3}\\ &= cT((x,y)) \end{aligned}T(c(x,y))​=T((cx,cy)))=3(cx)3+(cy)3​=3c3(x3+y3)​=c3x3+y3​=cT((x,y))​

Check 2) Vector addition

Consider, (1,0),(0,1)∈R2(1,0), (0,1) \in\mathbb{R}^{2}(1,0),(0,1)∈R2. So,

T((1,0))=13+033=1\begin{aligned} T((1,0)) &= \sqrt[3]{1^3+0^3}\\ &= 1 \end{aligned}T((1,0))​=313+03​=1​

and,

T((0,1))=03+133=1.\begin{aligned} T((0,1)) &= \sqrt[3]{0^3+1^3}\\ &= 1. \end{aligned}T((0,1))​=303+13​=1.​

Therefore,

T((1,0))+T((0,1))=1+1=2.\begin{aligned} T((1,0)) + T((0,1)) &= 1 + 1\\ &= 2. \end{aligned}T((1,0))+T((0,1))​=1+1=2.​

Also,

T((1,0)+(0,1))=T((1,1))=13+133=23.\begin{aligned} T((1,0) + (0,1)) &= T((1,1))\\ &= \sqrt[3]{1^3+1^3}\\ &= \sqrt[3]{2}. \end{aligned}T((1,0)+(0,1))​=T((1,1))=313+13​=32​.​

Since we have T((1,0))+T((0,1))≠T((1,0)+(0,1))T((1,0)) + T((0,1)) \neq T((1,0) + (0,1))T((1,0))+T((0,1))=T((1,0)+(0,1)), TTT doesn't preserve vector addition, thus it's not a linear transformation.

Linear Transformations as Matrices

Associated with any linear transformation T,T:Rn→RmT, T:R^{n} \rightarrow R^{m}T,T:Rn→Rm, is a matrix called the matrix of TTT.

Theorem: If AAA is a m×nm \times nm×n matrix, then TA:Rn→RmT_{A}: R^{n} \rightarrow R^{m}TA​:Rn→Rm defined by TA(v)=AvT_{A}(\mathbf{ v}) = A\mathbf{ v}TA​(v)=Av is a linear transformation.

Proof:

Check 1) Scalar multiplication

TA(cv)=A(cv)=cAv=cTA(v)\begin{aligned} T_{A}(c\mathbf{ v}) &= A(c\mathbf{ v})\\ &= cA\mathbf{ v}\\ &= cT_{A}(\mathbf{ v}) \end{aligned}TA​(cv)​=A(cv)=cAv=cTA​(v)​

Check 2) Vector addition

TA(u+v)=A(u+v)=A(u)+A(v)=TA(u)+TA(v)\begin{aligned} T_{A}(\mathbf{ u}+\mathbf{ v}) &= A(\mathbf{ u}+\mathbf{ v})\\ &= A(\mathbf{ u}) + A(\mathbf{ v})\\ &= T_{A}(\mathbf{ u}) + T_{A}(\mathbf{ v}) \end{aligned}TA​(u+v)​=A(u+v)=A(u)+A(v)=TA​(u)+TA​(v)​
■\blacksquare■

Example: Consider A=[111−1]A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1\\ 1 & -1\end{bmatrix}A=[11​1−1​]. So,

TA:R2→R2,TA((x,y))=A[xy]=[111−1][xy]=[x+yx−y]=(x+y,x−y).\begin{aligned} T_{A}: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}, T_{A}((x,y)) = A\begin{bmatrix} x\\ y\end{bmatrix}&=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1\\ 1 & -1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x\\ y\end{bmatrix}\\ &= \begin{bmatrix}x + y\\ x - y\end{bmatrix}\\ &= (x+y,x-y). \end{aligned}TA​:R2→R2,TA​((x,y))=A[xy​]​=[11​1−1​][xy​]=[x+yx−y​]=(x+y,x−y).​

Example: Consider A=[213745]A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1\\ 3 & 7\\ 4 & 5\end{bmatrix}A=⎣⎡​234​175​⎦⎤​. So,

TA:R2→R3,TA((x,y))=A[xy]=[213745][xy]=[2x+y3x+7y4x+5y]=(2x+y,3x+7y,4x+5y).\begin{aligned} T_{A}: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{3}, T_{A}((x,y)) = A\begin{bmatrix}x\\ y\end{bmatrix}&=\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1\\ 3 & 7\\ 4 & 5\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x\\ y\end{bmatrix}\\ &= \begin{bmatrix}2x + y\\ 3x + 7y\\ 4x + 5y\end{bmatrix}\\ &= (2x+y,3x+7y,4x+5y). \end{aligned}TA​:R2→R3,TA​((x,y))=A[xy​]​=⎣⎡​234​175​⎦⎤​[xy​]=⎣⎡​2x+y3x+7y4x+5y​⎦⎤​=(2x+y,3x+7y,4x+5y).​

Definition: Let T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}T:Rn→Rm be a linear transformation. The matrix associated with TTT is,

A=[T(e1)T(e2)T(e3)…T(en)] A = \begin{bmatrix}T(\mathbf{ e}_{1}) & T(\mathbf{ e}_{2}) & T(\mathbf{ e}_{3}) & \dots & T(\mathbf{ e}_{n})\end{bmatrix} A=[T(e1​)​T(e2​)​T(e3​)​…​T(en​)​]

where e1,e2,e3,…,en\mathbf{ e}_{1}, \mathbf{ e}_{2}, \mathbf{ e}_{3}, \dots, \mathbf{ e}_{n}e1​,e2​,e3​,…,en​ are the standard basis vectors of Rn\mathbb{R}^{n}Rn.

Example: T((x,y,z))=(2x−4y+3z,7x−2z)T((x,y,z)) = (2x-4y+3z,7x-2z)T((x,y,z))=(2x−4y+3z,7x−2z)

A=[T(1,0,0)T(0,1,0)T(0,0,1)]=[2−4370−2]\begin{aligned} A &= \begin{bmatrix}T(1,0,0) & T(0,1,0) & T(0,0,1)\end{bmatrix}\\ &= \begin{bmatrix}2 & -4 & 3\\ 7 & 0 & -2\end{bmatrix}\\ \end{aligned}A​=[T(1,0,0)​T(0,1,0)​T(0,0,1)​]=[27​−40​3−2​]​
TA:R3→R2T_{A}: \mathbb{R}^{3} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}TA​:R3→R2
TA((x,y,z))=[2−4370−2][xyz]=[2x−4y+3z7x−2z]=(2x−4y+3z,7x−2z)\begin{aligned} T_{A}((x,y,z)) &= \begin{bmatrix}2 & -4 & 3\\ 7 & 0 & -2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x\\ y\\ z\end{bmatrix}\\ &= \begin{bmatrix}2x-4y+3z\\ 7x-2z\end{bmatrix}\\ &= (2x-4y+3z,7x-2z) \end{aligned}TA​((x,y,z))​=[27​−40​3−2​]⎣⎡​xyz​⎦⎤​=[2x−4y+3z7x−2z​]=(2x−4y+3z,7x−2z)​

Notice this is equal to TTT, therefore TA=TT_{A} = TTA​=T.

The Image and Kernel of a Linear Transformation

Observe T:V→WT: V \rightarrow WT:V→W where VVV is the domain and WWW is the codomain.

Definition: The image of TTT (also known as the range of TTT) is

img(T)={Tv:v∈V}. \text{img}(T) = \{T\mathbf{ v}:\mathbf{ v}\in V\}. img(T)={Tv:v∈V}.

Definition: The kernel of TTT (also known as the nullspace of TTT) is

ker(T)={Tv=0:v∈V}. \text{ker}(T) = \{T\mathbf{ v}=\mathbf{ 0}:\mathbf{ v}\in V\}. ker(T)={Tv=0:v∈V}.

Theorem: A linear transformation must take the zero vector of VVV to the zero vector of WWW.

Proof:

T(0V)=T(0×0V)=0T(0V)=0W\begin{aligned} T(\mathbf{ 0}_{V}) &= T(0\times\mathbf{ 0}_{V})\\ &= 0T(\mathbf{ 0}_{V})\\ &= \mathbf{ 0}_{W} \end{aligned}T(0V​)​=T(0×0V​)=0T(0V​)=0W​​
■\blacksquare■

Non-Example: T((x,y))=x2+y2+1T((x,y)) = x^{2} + y^{2} + 1T((x,y))=x2+y2+1

This is not a linear transformation because T((0,0))=0+0+1=1T((0,0)) = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1T((0,0))=0+0+1=1 which is not the zero vector.

Non-Example: T:M2(R)→M2(R),T([abcd])=[dcba+1]T: M_{2}(\mathbb{R}) \rightarrow M_{2}(\mathbb{R}), T\left(\begin{bmatrix}a & b\\ c & d\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}d & c\\ b & a+1\end{bmatrix}T:M2​(R)→M2​(R),T([ac​bd​])=[db​ca+1​]

This is not a linear transformation because

T([0000])=[0001]. T\left(\begin{bmatrix}0 & 0\\ 0 & 0\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}. T([00​00​])=[00​01​].

Clearly, this is not the zero vector.

Observe that

ker(T)={Tv=0:v∈V}⊆Vimg(T)={Tv:v∈V}⊆W.\begin{aligned} \text{ker}(T) = \{T\mathbf{ v}=\mathbf{ 0}:\mathbf{ v}\in V\}\subseteq{V}\\ \text{img}(T) = \{T\mathbf{ v}:\mathbf{ v}\in V\}\subseteq{W}. \end{aligned}ker(T)={Tv=0:v∈V}⊆Vimg(T)={Tv:v∈V}⊆W.​

Theorem: ker(T)\text{ker}(T)ker(T) is a subspace of VVV.

Proof: We want to show three things:

  • 0∈ker(T)\mathbf{ 0}\in\text{ker}(T)0∈ker(T)

  • ker(T)\text{ker}(T)ker(T) is closed under scalar multiplication

  • ker(T)\text{ker}(T)ker(T) is closed under vector addition.

Check 1) We proved T(0V)=0WT(\mathbf{ 0}_{V}) = \mathbf{ 0}_{W}T(0V​)=0W​, so 0∈ker(T)\mathbf{ 0}\in\text{ker}(T)0∈ker(T).

Check 2) Take u∈ker(T),c∈R\mathbf{ u}\in\text{ker}(T), c\in\mathbb{R}u∈ker(T),c∈R. So, T(u)=0T(\mathbf{ u})=\mathbf{ 0}T(u)=0. Then,

T(cu)=cT(u)=c0=0.\begin{aligned} T(c\mathbf{ u}) &= cT(\mathbf{ u})\\ &= c\mathbf{ 0}\\ &= \mathbf{ 0}. \end{aligned}T(cu)​=cT(u)=c0=0.​

Thus, cu∈ker(T)c\mathbf{ u}\in\text{ker}(T)cu∈ker(T).

Check 3) Take u,v∈ker(T)u,v\in\text{ker}(T)u,v∈ker(T). So, T(u)=0T(\mathbf{ u})=\mathbf{ 0}T(u)=0 and T(v)=0T(\mathbf{ v})=\mathbf{ 0}T(v)=0. Then,

T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v)=0+0=0.\begin{aligned} T(\mathbf{ u}+\mathbf{ v}) &= T(\mathbf{ u}) + T(\mathbf{ v})\\ &= \mathbf{ 0} + \mathbf{ 0}\\ &= \mathbf{ 0}. \end{aligned}T(u+v)​=T(u)+T(v)=0+0=0.​

Thus, u+v∈ker(T)\mathbf{ u} + \mathbf{ v} \in \text{ker}(T)u+v∈ker(T).

■\blacksquare■

Example: T:P2(R)→R2,T(a+bx+cx2)=(a−b,a+c)T: P_{2}(\mathbb{R}) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{2}, T(a+bx+cx^{2}) = (a-b,a+c)T:P2​(R)→R2,T(a+bx+cx2)=(a−b,a+c)

Note: P2(R)={p(x)is a polynomial of degree at most 2}P_{2}(\mathbb{R}) = \{p(x) \text{is a polynomial of degree at most } 2\}P2​(R)={p(x)is a polynomial of degree at most 2}

ker(T)={p(x)∈P2(R)∣T(p(x))=(0,0)}={a+ax−ax2∣a∈R}=span{1+x−x2}\begin{aligned} \text{ker}(T) &= \{p(x)\in P_{2}(\mathbb{R})| T(p(x)) = (0,0)\}\\ &= \{a + ax - ax^{2}| a\in\mathbb{R}\}\\ &= \text{span}\{1+x-x^{2}\} \end{aligned}ker(T)​={p(x)∈P2​(R)∣T(p(x))=(0,0)}={a+ax−ax2∣a∈R}=span{1+x−x2}​

Example: D:C′(I)→C0(I),D(f(x))=f′(x)D: C'(I) \rightarrow C^{0}(I), D(f(x)) = f'(x)D:C′(I)→C0(I),D(f(x))=f′(x)

ker(D)={f(x)∈C1(I)∣D(f(x))=0}={f(x)∈C1(I)∣f′(x)=0}={f(x)∈C1(I)∣f(x)=a for some a∈R}\begin{aligned} \text{ker}(D) &= \{f(x)\in C^{1}(I) | D(f(x))=0\}\\ &= \{f(x)\in C^{1}(I) | f'(x)=0\}\\ &= \{f(x)\in C^{1}(I) | f(x) = a\text{ for some } a\in\mathbb{R}\} \end{aligned}ker(D)​={f(x)∈C1(I)∣D(f(x))=0}={f(x)∈C1(I)∣f′(x)=0}={f(x)∈C1(I)∣f(x)=a for some a∈R}​

Example: T:C2(I)→C0(I),T(f(x))=f′′(x)−f(x)T:C^{2}(I) \rightarrow C^{0}(I), T(f(x)) = f''(x)-f(x)T:C2(I)→C0(I),T(f(x))=f′′(x)−f(x)

ker(T)={f∈C2(I)∣T(f)=0}={f∈C2(I)∣f′′(x)−f(x)=0}={f∈C2(I)∣f′′(x)=f(x)}={C1ex+C2e−x∣C1,C2∈R}\begin{aligned} \text{ker}(T) &= \{f\in C^{2}(I)|T(f)=0\}\\ &= \{f\in C^{2}(I)|f''(x)-f(x)=0\}\\ &= \{f\in C^{2}(I)|f''(x)=f(x)\}\\ &= \{C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{x}+C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{-x}| C_{1}, C{2}\in\mathbb{R}\} \end{aligned}ker(T)​={f∈C2(I)∣T(f)=0}={f∈C2(I)∣f′′(x)−f(x)=0}={f∈C2(I)∣f′′(x)=f(x)}={C1​ex+C2​e−x∣C1​,C2∈R}​

Second-Order Linear Ordinary Differential Equations

Definition: A second-order linear ODE is an equation of the form

a(x)y′′+b(x)y′+c(x)y=f(x) a(x)y'' + b(x)y' + c(x)y = f(x) a(x)y′′+b(x)y′+c(x)y=f(x)

where a(x),b(x),c(x)a(x),b(x),c(x)a(x),b(x),c(x) are known as coefficient functions and f(x)f(x)f(x) is known as the forcing function. This is homogeneous if f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0, and has constant coefficients if a(x),b(x),c(x)a(x),b(x),c(x)a(x),b(x),c(x) are all constant.

Example: y′′+0y′−y=0y'' + 0y' - y = 0y′′+0y′−y=0

So, y′′=yy'' = yy′′=y with solutions ex\mathrm{e}^{x}ex and e−x\mathrm{e}^{-x}e−x.

Example: y′′+0y′−4y=0y'' + 0y' - 4y = 0y′′+0y′−4y=0

So, y′′=4yy'' = 4yy′′=4y with solutions e2x\mathrm{e}^{2x}e2x and e−2x\mathrm{e}^{-2x}e−2x.

Example: y′′+3y′+2y=0y'' + 3y' + 2y = 0y′′+3y′+2y=0

Guess y=erxy = \mathrm{e}^{rx}y=erx.

(erx)′′+3(erx)′+2(erx)=0r2erx+3rerx+2erx=0(r2+3r+2)erx=0r2+3r+2=0(r+2)(r+1)=0\begin{aligned} (\mathrm{e}^{rx})'' + 3(\mathrm{e}^{rx})' + 2(\mathrm{e}^{rx}) &= 0\\ r^{2}\mathrm{e}^{rx} + 3r\mathrm{e}^{rx} + 2\mathrm{e}^{rx} &= 0\\ (r^{2}+3r+2)\mathrm{e}^{rx} &= 0\\ r^{2} + 3r + 2 &= 0\\ (r+2)(r+1) &= 0\\ \end{aligned}(erx)′′+3(erx)′+2(erx)r2erx+3rerx+2erx(r2+3r+2)erxr2+3r+2(r+2)(r+1)​=0=0=0=0=0​

So,

r=−2,r=−1 r = -2, r = -1 r=−2,r=−1

which means

y=e−2x,y=e−x. y = \mathrm{e}^{-2x}, y = \mathrm{e}^{-x}. y=e−2x,y=e−x.

Note that any linear combination of the two is also a solution. Thus, our general solution is

y=C1e−2x+C2e−x. y = C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-2x} + C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{-x}. y=C1​e−2x+C2​e−x.

Example: y′′−y′−6y=0y'' - y' - 6y = 0y′′−y′−6y=0

Guess y=erxy = \mathrm{e}^{rx}y=erx.

(erx)′′−(erx)′−6(erx)=0r2erx−rerx−6erx=0(r2−r−6)erx=0r2−r−6=0(r+2)(r−3)=0\begin{aligned} (\mathrm{e}^{rx})'' - (\mathrm{e}^{rx})' - 6(\mathrm{e}^{rx}) &= 0\\ r^{2}\mathrm{e}^{rx} - r\mathrm{e}^{rx} - 6\mathrm{e}^{rx} &= 0\\ (r^{2}-r-6)\mathrm{e}^{rx} &= 0\\ r^{2} - r - 6 &= 0\\ (r+2)(r-3) &= 0\\ \end{aligned}(erx)′′−(erx)′−6(erx)r2erx−rerx−6erx(r2−r−6)erxr2−r−6(r+2)(r−3)​=0=0=0=0=0​

So,

r=−2,r=3 r = -2, r = 3 r=−2,r=3

which means

y=e−2x,y=e3x. y = \mathrm{e}^{-2x}, y = \mathrm{e}^{3x}. y=e−2x,y=e3x.

Thus, our general solution is

y=C1e−2x+C2e3x. y = C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-2x} + C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{3x}. y=C1​e−2x+C2​e3x.

Definition: The characteristic polynomial of the second-order differential equation

ay′′+by′+cy=0 ay'' + by' + cy = 0 ay′′+by′+cy=0

is the polynomial

p(r)=ar2+br+c. p(r) = ar^{2} + br + c. p(r)=ar2+br+c.

Applications

Mass-spring system

The equilibrium length of a mass-spring system is denoted as lll. The distance of the mass to the system's equilibrium is denoted as yyy. The force of gravity is denoted as FgF_{g}Fg​ while the force of the spring is denoted as FsF_{s}Fs​. Respectively, Fg=mgF_{g} = mgFg​=mg and Fs=−k(y−l)F_{s} = -k(y-l)Fs​=−k(y−l). So, the total force exerted on a mass in a mass-spring system is represented by the equation,

Ft=−k(y−l). F_{t} = -k(y-l). Ft​=−k(y−l).

However, notice that all forces are of the form F=maF=maF=ma, so

−k(y−l)=my′′. -k(y-l) = my''. −k(y−l)=my′′.

Moving the left-hand side to the right means we have the differential equation

my′′+k(y−l)=0. my'' + k(y-l) = 0. my′′+k(y−l)=0.

Let y~=y−l\tilde{y} = y-ly~​=y−l (relative length). Now the differential equation is

my~′′+ky~=0. m\tilde{y}'' + k\tilde{y} = 0. my~​′′+ky~​=0.

Pendulum

From the second-order differential equation

d2θdt2+glsin⁡(θ)=0, \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}\theta}{\mathrm{d} t^{2}} + \frac{g}{l}\sin(\theta) = 0, dt2d2θ​+lg​sin(θ)=0,

we can use the small-angle approximation where sin⁡(θ)≈θ\sin(\theta) \approx \thetasin(θ)≈θ to linearize our original differential equation as

d2θdt2+glθ=0. \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}\theta}{\mathrm{d} t^{2}} + \frac{g}{l}\theta = 0. dt2d2θ​+lg​θ=0.

LCR-circuit

LQ′′+RQ′+1CQ=0my~′′+γy~′+ky~′=0\begin{aligned} LQ'' + RQ' + \frac{1}{C}Q &= 0\\ m\tilde{y}'' + \gamma\tilde{y}' + k\tilde{y}' &= 0 \end{aligned}LQ′′+RQ′+C1​Qmy~​′′+γy~​′+ky~​′​=0=0​

Linear Ordinary Differential Equations

Definition: A nth\mathbf n^{\textbf{th}}nth-order linear ODE is an equation of the form

an(x)y(n)+an−1(x)y(n−1)+an−2(x)y(n−2)+⋯+a1(x)y′+a0(x)y=f(x) a_{n}(x)y^{(n)} + a_{n-1}(x)y^{(n-1)} + a_{n-2}(x)y^{(n-2)} + \dots + a_{1}(x)y' + a_{0}(x)y = f(x) an​(x)y(n)+an−1​(x)y(n−1)+an−2​(x)y(n−2)+⋯+a1​(x)y′+a0​(x)y=f(x)

where an(x),an−1(x),an−2(x),…,a1(x),a0(x)a_{n}(x), a_{n-1}(x), a_{n-2}(x), \dots, a_{1}(x), a_{0}(x)an​(x),an−1​(x),an−2​(x),…,a1​(x),a0​(x) are known as coefficient functions and f(x)f(x)f(x) is known as the forcing function.

There's an important special case when f(x)=0f(x) = 0f(x)=0, we call this equation homogeneous, for example,

my′′+γy′+ky=0. my'' + \gamma y' + ky = 0. my′′+γy′+ky=0.

Initial Conditions

An initial condition for a nthn^{\text{th}}nth-order ODE is of the form

y(x0)=y0,y′(x0)=y1,y′′(x0)=y2,…,yn−1(x0)=yn−1. y(x_{0}) = y_{0}, y'(x_{0}) = y_{1}, y''(x_{0}) = y_{2}, \dots, y^{n-1}(x_{0}) = y_{n-1}. y(x0​)=y0​,y′(x0​)=y1​,y′′(x0​)=y2​,…,yn−1(x0​)=yn−1​.

An ODE with an initial condition is called an initial value problem (IVP).

Existence and Uniqueness Theorem

Consider a nthn^{\text{th}}nth-order linear initial value problem

an(x)y(n)+an−1(x)y(n−1)+an−2(x)y(n−2)+⋯+a1(x)y′+a0(x)y=f(x) a_{n}(x)y^{(n)} + a_{n-1}(x)y^{(n-1)} + a_{n-2}(x)y^{(n-2)} + \dots + a_{1}(x)y' + a_{0}(x)y = f(x) an​(x)y(n)+an−1​(x)y(n−1)+an−2​(x)y(n−2)+⋯+a1​(x)y′+a0​(x)y=f(x)

with y(x0)=y0,y′(x0)=y1,y′′(x0)=y2,…,y(n−1)(x0)=yn−1y(x_{0}) = y_{0}, y'(x_{0}) = y_{1}, y''(x_{0}) = y_{2}, \dots, y^{(n-1)}(x_{0}) = y_{n-1}y(x0​)=y0​,y′(x0​)=y1​,y′′(x0​)=y2​,…,y(n−1)(x0​)=yn−1​.

Observe that if

  • III is an interval containing x0x_{0}x0​

  • a0(x),…,an(x),f(x)a_{0}(x), \dots, a_{n}(x), f(x)a0​(x),…,an​(x),f(x) are continuous on III

  • an(x)≠0a_{n}(x) \neq 0an​(x)=0 on III

then the initial value problem has a unique solution on all of III.

Example: y′′+5y′+4y=0,y(0)=1,y′(0)=3y'' + 5y' + 4y = 0, y(0) = 1, y'(0) = 3y′′+5y′+4y=0,y(0)=1,y′(0)=3

Guess y=erty = \mathrm{e}^{rt}y=ert.

(ert)′′+5(ert)′+4(ert)=0r2ert+5rert+4ert=0(r2+5r+4)ert=0r2+5r+4=0(r+4)(r+1)=0\begin{aligned} (\mathrm{e}^{rt})'' + 5(\mathrm{e}^{rt})' + 4(\mathrm{e}^{rt}) &= 0\\ r^{2}\mathrm{e}^{rt} + 5r\mathrm{e}^{rt} + 4\mathrm{e}^{rt} &= 0\\ (r^{2}+5r+4)\mathrm{e}^{rt} &= 0\\ r^{2} + 5r + 4 &= 0\\ (r+4)(r+1) &= 0 \end{aligned}(ert)′′+5(ert)′+4(ert)r2ert+5rert+4ert(r2+5r+4)ertr2+5r+4(r+4)(r+1)​=0=0=0=0=0​

So,

r=−4,r=−1 r = -4, r = -1 r=−4,r=−1

which means

y=e−4t,y=e−t. y = \mathrm{e}^{-4t}, y = \mathrm{e}^{-t}. y=e−4t,y=e−t.

Therefore, our general solution is

y=C1e−4t+C2e−t. y = C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-4t} + C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{-t}. y=C1​e−4t+C2​e−t.

Using y(0)=1y(0) = 1y(0)=1 we have,

1=C1e0+C2e01=C1+C2.\begin{aligned} 1 &= C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{0} + C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{0}\\ 1 &= C_{1} + C_{2}. \end{aligned}11​=C1​e0+C2​e0=C1​+C2​.​

Taking the derivative of yyy we have,

y′=−4C1e−4t−C2e−t. y' = -4C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-4t} - C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{-t}. y′=−4C1​e−4t−C2​e−t.

Using y′(0)=3y'(0) = 3y′(0)=3 we have,

3=−4C1e0−C2e03=−4C1−C2.\begin{aligned} 3 &= -4C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{0} - C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{0}\\ 3 &= -4C_{1} - C_{2}. \end{aligned}33​=−4C1​e0−C2​e0=−4C1​−C2​.​

Now, we have the equations

1=C1+C23=−4C1−C2\begin{aligned} 1 &= C_{1} + C_{2}\\ 3 &= -4C_{1} - C_{2} \end{aligned}13​=C1​+C2​=−4C1​−C2​​

so

4=−3C1C1=−43C2=1−(−43)C2=73.\begin{aligned} 4 &= -3C_{1}\\ C_{1} &= -\frac{4}{3}\\ C_{2} &= 1 - \left(-\frac{4}{3}\right)\\ C_{2} &= \frac{7}{3}. \end{aligned}4C1​C2​C2​​=−3C1​=−34​=1−(−34​)=37​.​

Therefore, our solution to the initial value problem is

y=−43e−4t+73e−t. y = -\frac{4}{3}\mathrm{e}^{-4t} + \frac{7}{3}\mathrm{e}^{-t}. y=−34​e−4t+37​e−t.

Example: t2y′′+3ty′+y=0,y(1)=7,y′(1)=2t^{2}y'' + 3ty' + y = 0, y(1) = 7, y'(1) = 2t2y′′+3ty′+y=0,y(1)=7,y′(1)=2

Guess y=try = t^{r}y=tr.

t2(tr)′′+3t(tr)′+(tr)=0t2(r(r−1)tr−2)+3t(rtr−1)+tr=0r(r−1)tr+3rtr+tr=0(r2+2r+1)tr=0r2+2r+1=0(r+1)(r+1)=0\begin{aligned} t^{2}(t^{r})'' + 3t(t^{r})' + (t^{r}) &= 0\\ t^{2}(r(r-1)t^{r-2}) + 3t(rt^{r-1}) + t^{r} &= 0\\ r(r-1)t^{r} + 3rt^{r} + t^{r} &= 0\\ (r^{2}+2r+1)t^{r} &= 0\\ r^{2} + 2r + 1 &= 0\\ (r+1)(r+1) &= 0 \end{aligned}t2(tr)′′+3t(tr)′+(tr)t2(r(r−1)tr−2)+3t(rtr−1)+trr(r−1)tr+3rtr+tr(r2+2r+1)trr2+2r+1(r+1)(r+1)​=0=0=0=0=0=0​

So,

r=−1,r=−1 r = -1, r = -1 r=−1,r=−1

which means

y=t−1,y=t−1. y = t^{-1}, y = t^{-1}. y=t−1,y=t−1.

However, our true second solution is

y=t−1ln⁡t. y = t^{-1}\ln{t}. y=t−1lnt.

Therefore, our general solution is

y=C1t−1+C2t−1ln⁡t. y = C_{1}t^{-1} + C_{2}t^{-1}\ln{t}. y=C1​t−1+C2​t−1lnt.

Using y(1)=7y(1) = 7y(1)=7 we have,

7=C11−1+C21−1ln⁡(1)C1=7.\begin{aligned} 7 &= C_{1}1^{-1} + C_{2}1^{-1}\ln{(1)}\\ C_{1} &= 7. \end{aligned}7C1​​=C1​1−1+C2​1−1ln(1)=7.​

Taking the derivative of yyy we have,

y′=−C1t−2−C2t−2ln⁡t+C2t−2=−C1t−2+C2(−t−2ln⁡t+t−2).\begin{aligned} y' &= -C_{1}t^{-2} - C_{2}t^{-2}\ln{t} + C_{2}t^{-2}\\ &= -C_{1}t^{-2} + C_{2}(-t^{-2}\ln{t}+t^{-2}). \end{aligned}y′​=−C1​t−2−C2​t−2lnt+C2​t−2=−C1​t−2+C2​(−t−2lnt+t−2).​

Using y′(1)=2y'(1) = 2y′(1)=2 we have,

2=−C11−2+C2(−1−2ln⁡1+1−2)2=−C1+C2C2=9.\begin{aligned} 2 &= -C_{1}1^{-2} + C_{2}(-1^{-2}\ln{1}+1^{-2})\\ 2 &= -C_{1} + C_{2}\\ C_{2} &= 9. \end{aligned}22C2​​=−C1​1−2+C2​(−1−2ln1+1−2)=−C1​+C2​=9.​

Therefore, our solution to the initial value problem is

y=7t−1+9t−1ln⁡t. y = 7t^{-1} + 9t^{-1}\ln{t}. y=7t−1+9t−1lnt.

Example: y′′′−y′=0,y(0)=8,y′(0)=3,y′′(0)=0y''' - y' = 0, y(0) = 8, y'(0) = 3, y''(0) = 0y′′′−y′=0,y(0)=8,y′(0)=3,y′′(0)=0

Guess y=erty = \mathrm{e}^{rt}y=ert.

(ert)′′′−(ert)′=0r3ert−rert=0ert(r3−r)=0r(r+1)(r−1)=0\begin{aligned} (\mathrm{e}^{rt})''' - (\mathrm{e}^{rt})' &= 0\\ r^{3}\mathrm{e}^{rt} - r\mathrm{e}^{rt} &= 0\\ \mathrm{e}^{rt}(r^{3}-r) &= 0\\ r(r+1)(r-1) &= 0 \end{aligned}(ert)′′′−(ert)′r3ert−rertert(r3−r)r(r+1)(r−1)​=0=0=0=0​

So,

r=−1,r=0,r=1. r = -1, r = 0, r = 1. r=−1,r=0,r=1.

Therefore, our general solution is

y=C1e−1t+C2e0t+C3e1t=C1e−t+C2+C3et.\begin{aligned} y &= C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-1t} + C_{2}\mathrm{e}^{0t} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{1t}\\ &= C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-t} + C_{2} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{t}. \end{aligned}y​=C1​e−1t+C2​e0t+C3​e1t=C1​e−t+C2​+C3​et.​

Using y(0)=8y(0) = 8y(0)=8 we have,

8=C1e0+C2+C3e0=C1+C2+C3.\begin{aligned} 8 &= C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{0} + C_{2} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{0}\\ &= C_{1} + C_{2} + C_{3}. \end{aligned}8​=C1​e0+C2​+C3​e0=C1​+C2​+C3​.​

Taking the derivative of yyy we have,

y′=−C1e−t+C3et. y' = -C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-t} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{t}. y′=−C1​e−t+C3​et.

Using y′(0)=3y'(0) = 3y′(0)=3 we have,

3=−C1e0+C3e0=−C1+C3.\begin{aligned} 3 &= -C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{0} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{0}\\ &= -C_{1} + C_{3}. \end{aligned}3​=−C1​e0+C3​e0=−C1​+C3​.​

Taking the second derivative of yyy we have,

y′′=C1e−t+C3et. y'' = C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{-t} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{t}. y′′=C1​e−t+C3​et.

Using y′′(0)=0y''(0) = 0y′′(0)=0 we have,

0=C1e0+C3e0=C1+C3.\begin{aligned} 0 &= C_{1}\mathrm{e}^{0} + C_{3}\mathrm{e}^{0}\\ &= C_{1} + C_{3}. \end{aligned}0​=C1​e0+C3​e0=C1​+C3​.​

So,

C1=−C3. C_{1} = -C_{3}. C1​=−C3​.

From

3=−C1+C3 3 = -C_{1} + C_{3} 3=−C1​+C3​

we have,

3=2C3C3=32\begin{aligned} 3 &= 2C_{3}\\ C_{3} &= \frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}3C3​​=2C3​=23​​

which means

C1=−32. C_{1} = -\frac{3}{2}. C1​=−23​.

Thus,

8=C1+C2+C3=−32+C2+32=C2.\begin{aligned} 8 &= C_{1} + C_{2} + C_{3}\\ &= -\frac{3}{2} + C_{2} + \frac{3}{2}\\ &= C_{2}. \end{aligned}8​=C1​+C2​+C3​=−23​+C2​+23​=C2​.​

Therefore, our solution to the initial value problem is

y=−32e−t+8+32et. y = -\frac{3}{2}\mathrm{e}^{-t} + 8 + \frac{3}{2}\mathrm{e}^{t}. y=−23​e−t+8+23​et.

Definition: A linear ODE is regular on III if

  • a0(x),…,an(x),f(x)a_{0}(x), \dots, a_{n}(x), f(x)a0​(x),…,an​(x),f(x) are continuous on III

  • an(x)≠0a_{n}(x) \neq 0an​(x)=0 for all x∈Ix\in Ix∈I.

Theorem: The set of all solutions to a homogeneous nthn^{\text{th}}nth-order linear ODE which is regular on III is a nnn-dimensional subspace of Cn(I)C^{n}(I)Cn(I).

Corollary: The general solution of a nthn^{\text{th}}nth-order differential equation should have nnn constants.

Theorem: If y=f1(x),y=f2(x),y=f3(x),…,y=fm(x)y = f_{1}(x), y = f_{2}(x), y = f_{3}(x), \dots, y = f_{m}(x)y=f1​(x),y=f2​(x),y=f3​(x),…,y=fm​(x) are all solutions to the linear differential equation

an(x)y(n)+an−1(x)y(n−1)+an−2(x)y(n−2)+⋯+a1(x)y′+a0(x)y=f(x) a_{n}(x)y^{(n)} + a_{n-1}(x)y^{(n-1)} + a_{n-2}(x)y^{(n-2)} + \dots + a_{1}(x)y' + a_{0}(x)y = f(x) an​(x)y(n)+an−1​(x)y(n−1)+an−2​(x)y(n−2)+⋯+a1​(x)y′+a0​(x)y=f(x)

which is regular on III, then for some x∈Ix\in Ix∈I, W(f1(x),…,fm(x))≠0W(f_{1}(x),\dots,f_{m}(x)) \neq 0W(f1​(x),…,fm​(x))=0 if and only if f1(x),…,fm(x)f_{1}(x),\dots,f_{m}(x)f1​(x),…,fm​(x) are linearly independent in Cn(I)C^{n}(I)Cn(I).

Complex Roots

Recall Euler's formula

eiθ=cos⁡(θ)+isin⁡(θ) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\theta} = \cos(\theta) + \mathrm{i}\sin(\theta) eiθ=cos(θ)+isin(θ)

and the quadratic formula for finding roots

r=−b±b2−4ac2a. r = \frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^{2}-4ac}}{2a}. r=2a−b±b2−4ac​​.
